step_1_context.text: With any of, each of, either of, neither of, or none of and a plural noun / pronoun we can use a singular or plural verb. (We use a singular verb in formal written English.)

step_2_explanation.simple_rule: With any of, each of, either of, neither of, or none of and a plural noun / pronoun we can use a singular or plural verb.
step_2_explanation.formula:
When a subject has two or more items joined by and, we usually use a plural verb:
 • Ingrid and Tobias are moving back to Australia.
step_2_explanation.common_mistake: When a subject is made up of two or more items joined by (either).

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 41.1.1 I'd be surprised if any of my children ___ my birthday.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: With 'any of' + plural noun, we can use a plural or singular verb (singular is more formal).

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 41.1.2 It is estimated that the number of ___ of the flooding ___ 100,000.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Use 'the number of' + plural noun + singular verb.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 41.2.1 Plenty of jobs ___ available, but no one ___ them because they're so poorly paid. (be / want)
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: 'Plenty of' + plural noun takes a plural verb. 'No one' takes a singular verb.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 41.2.2 The majority of those questioned ___ that the government's economic policies have failed. (think)
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: 'The majority of' + plural noun takes a plural verb.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 41.2.3 Professor Smith and Dr Peters ___ that the wreck of the ship and its cargo ___ a danger to local people fishing near the island. (claim / constitute)
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Two items joined by 'and' take a plural verb ('claim'). A compound subject thought of as a single unit ('wreck and its cargo') can take a singular or plural verb.
